0x41434f

The Machine Doesn't Know

Jan 15, 2026

In January, a patient I knew died in their bed with a CPAP mask on their face.

I met this patient three times across three different units over the course of about a month. The first time was in mid-December. They were in restraints for being disruptive. I negotiated with the nurse to get them released, provided they stayed calm. They did. They paced the hallway and talked to me. They were convinced they had seen me before at another hospital. I do not think they had, but the certainty in their voice made me wonder.

The second time was the week of December 25. The hospital floated me to another unit, and I was assigned as the patient’s CPAP watcher for the night. They barely slept. Maybe thirty minutes the entire shift. The rest of the time, we talked. They told me they did not like using the machine. It was uncomfortable. Hard to move around with. You have to adjust the mask all night. Most patients feel the same way about CPAPs. They tolerate them, but barely.

The third time was in January, on yet another unit. The patient was angry. They had just been told their stay was being extended by 14 more days. I told them to talk to the psychiatrist, since the psychiatrist is the only one who can authorize a discharge. That was the last conversation we had. When my shift ended, the patient waved to me as I walked out.

A few days later, they died in their bed, and the machine kept running after they stopped breathing.

The doctors knew the patient had sleep apnea. The CPAP was at the bedside. A staff member was assigned to watch them. Everything was in place. But after the death, the hospital told us something we had not been told before: the patient had a prior code blue on record. I was never given that information when I was assigned as their CPAP watcher. I sat with this patient for an entire night shift, responsible for monitoring a machine I barely understood, attached to a patient whose cardiac history I did not know existed.

A CPAP machine blows air. That is what it does. In a dark room at night, the sound of the motor is constant. If the patient is breathing, the machine sounds one way. If the patient stops breathing, the machine sounds exactly the same. It does not alarm. It does not alert anyone. It just keeps pushing air into a body that is no longer alive.

As mental health workers, we are not trained on CPAP devices. We know how to plug them in, add water, and turn them on. The screens often go dark after a period of inactivity, and in a dim room, it is impossible to tell whether the machine is functioning correctly. I have seen staff press a button just to wake up the screen, only to accidentally shut the power off because the controls were confusing. We are responsible for monitoring patients on these machines, but we do not understand the machines themselves.

I was not in the unit when it happened. But I was in the building when the code blue was called. I was close to the action when the EMTs came. I did not see the patient. But I felt the shift in the air. The floor got quiet in a way it never gets quiet.

For about a week after, I was in strong disbelief. Not the kind where you question whether it happened. The kind where your mind keeps circling back to it and cannot land anywhere. I have experience with death. I lost my father on a Tuesday, and he had called me on a Sunday. The last time I heard his voice was two days before I lost him. This patient waved goodbye to me at the end of a shift, and a few days later was gone. I do not think I processed the patient’s death separately from my father’s. They layered on top of each other. The pattern was the same. A final, ordinary moment that you do not know is final until it is too late.

I talked to my therapist about it. I talked to my friend who works in social work. I thought about resigning. My original plan had been to leave after six months so I could free up time for studying, but for that week, the reason shifted. It was not about the schedule anymore. It was about whether I could carry this.

I stayed. And something changed in how I work.

Before this patient died, I used to feel guilty about going back to the nurses with observations. They are buried in administrative tasks at the computer, prepping discharge paperwork, intake documentation, flagging what the doctors need to sign, managing compliance requirements and insurance billing. In my opinion, much of this workload is maximized for compliance and payout rather than for patient care, though I stand to be corrected. Every time I walked up to report something a patient said or something I noticed on rounds, I felt like I was taking away from their time. After the death, I stopped feeling guilty. Now I think of that patient every time I do my rounds. If I see something, I report it. I do not care if the nurse is busy. The alternative is worse.

The hospital changed a lot of things after it happened. They fired the three staff members who signed the patient’s rounds from 11:30 PM onward that night. They changed the identification color for CPAP patients to yellow so they are immediately visible. They started requiring licensed staff to do one-to-one monitoring for every CPAP patient, where before an unlicensed mental health worker could be assigned. The house supervisor now rounds on CPAP patients directly. The shift supervisor does additional rounds every two hours on top of the assigned licensed staff.

They gave us handouts on sleep apnea and CPAP operation. They posted signs at the bedside showing how to properly fit the mask. We signed new documentation acknowledging we had been informed. They bought flashlights and pulse oximeters so staff could check on sleeping patients without relying on the sound of the machine. They purchased pillows designed for CPAP use. We now have to document in the electronic health record every time a patient takes the mask off and every time it goes back on.

Then the inspectors came. CMS and the state health department reviewed the case. The hospital prepped us for questions. One finding stood out. CMS noted that the patient’s blood pressure had appeared irregular, but there was no record of whether the mental health worker who took the reading had ever told a nurse. So now, every time we report a vital sign or an observation to an RN, we have to document which nurse we told, by name, in the system.

They also stopped hiring unlicensed mental health workers for new positions and posted registered nurse roles instead. They brought back licensed psychiatric technicians and licensed vocational nurses, roles they had eliminated years earlier because they wanted RNs to sign certain paperwork for insurance purposes. The death forced them to reconsider that calculation.

All of these changes happened after the patient died. Every single one of them could have existed before.

This is what I mean when I write about reactive systems. The protocols that are now standard on my floor, the pulse oximeters, the licensed one-to-ones, the documentation requirements, none of them required new technology. None of them required new legislation. They required a death. The system knew the risks. CPAP machines have always worked this way. Sleep apnea has always been dangerous. The gap between what we knew and what we did was not a knowledge gap. It was a policy gap. And a patient filled it with their life.

There is also an insurance dimension to this that I cannot ignore. CPAP machines are personal medical devices. Insurance companies do not easily replace them. Hospitals do not stock them. Patients are expected to bring their own. If a patient arrives without one, or if theirs is broken, the options are limited. We are a psychiatric hospital, not a respiratory clinic. But sleep apnea does not care what kind of hospital you are in. The separation between psychiatric care and medical care, the same separation I wrote about after my ambulance ride, is not just an intellectual problem. It is a gap that patients fall through. This patient fell through it.

I went online after the death looking for solutions. I found companies building at-home sleep tests that attach to your chest, measure breathing patterns, and send data to an app. They give you a score on how well you slept. But we do not need a sleep score. We need a machine that knows when the patient is dead.

This is the next project for Stratification Labs. I want to build an AI-powered portable CPAP with better safety features for institutional use. It needs a biological alarm that detects when the chest stops rising or the pulse stops and immediately sounds a distinct emergency alert, not just the steady hum of the motor. It needs to be safe for a psychiatric ward, with a hose connection redesigned so patients can move freely without creating a ligature risk. And it needs to be smarter than the machines we currently use. Right now, a CPAP runs the same whether the patient is alive or dead. That has to change.

I am not building this because I think technology solves everything. I have written enough essays by now to make clear that I do not believe that. I am building it because a patient I cared about died, and the machine at their bedside did not know.

© 2026 0x414344f

Software Engineer. Mental Health Worker. Building toward Computational Psychiatry.
